Keywords: confirmation, selective evidence, cherry-picking, disconfirming, preferred option, validation seeking
Description: The tendency to seek, interpret, and remember information that confirms pre-existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence.
Impact on Decisions: Causes decision-makers to build a one-sided case for their preferred option, systematically underweighting evidence against it.
How to Detect: Ask: Have we actively sought evidence AGAINST our preferred option? Are we explaining away contradictory data?
Debiasing Strategy: Assign a devil's advocate role. Explicitly seek disconfirming evidence for the leading option. Require equal analysis of all options.
Example: A team evaluating a new market entry only gathers data supporting the opportunity while dismissing negative market signals.

Keywords: anchoring, first impression, initial value, reference point, starting point, adjustment insufficient
Description: Over-weighting the first piece of information encountered (the anchor) and insufficiently adjusting from it when making subsequent judgments.
Impact on Decisions: First option presented, first number mentioned, or first opinion shared disproportionately influences the final decision.
How to Detect: Ask: Would our evaluation change if we had seen the options in a different order? Is the first proposal dominating the discussion?
Debiasing Strategy: Generate evaluation criteria BEFORE seeing any options. Use structured scoring independently before group discussion. Randomize option presentation order.
Example: A vendor's initial price quote becomes the anchor, and all negotiation revolves around that number rather than true market value.

Keywords: sunk cost, past investment, throwing good money after bad, committed, already spent, waste aversion
Description: Continuing with a failing course of action because of past investment (time, money, effort) rather than evaluating future value.
Impact on Decisions: Prevents abandoning failing options because 'we've already invested too much.' Past costs are irrelevant to future decisions but feel otherwise.
How to Detect: Ask: If we were starting fresh today with no prior investment, would we still choose this option? Are we justifying continuation by pointing to past spend?
Debiasing Strategy: Evaluate each option as if starting fresh today (zero-based evaluation). Separate the past investment discussion from the forward-looking decision.
Example: Continuing to develop a product feature that shows poor user adoption because the team has already spent 6 months building it.

Keywords: status quo, default, inertia, change aversion, current state, comfort zone, do nothing
Description: Preferring the current state of affairs simply because it is the current state, even when change would produce better outcomes.
Impact on Decisions: The status quo gets an unfair advantage in evaluation. Change requires a higher burden of proof than maintaining the current approach.
How to Detect: Ask: Are we holding the status quo to the same standard as alternatives? Would we actively choose the current approach if starting from scratch?
Debiasing Strategy: Explicitly include status quo as one option evaluated with the same rigor as all alternatives. Calculate the cost of inaction.
Example: A company continues using an outdated CRM system because switching feels risky, even though the annual cost of inefficiency exceeds migration cost.

Keywords: overconfidence, calibration, confidence interval, prediction accuracy, too certain, know-it-all, hubris
Description: Overestimating the accuracy of own predictions, knowledge, and judgment. Expressing certainty well beyond what evidence supports.
Impact on Decisions: Leads to too-narrow confidence intervals, insufficient contingency planning, and rejection of ambiguity that should inform the decision.
How to Detect: Ask: What is our confidence level, and what is it based on? Have we been right about similar predictions before? Are our confidence intervals wide enough?
Debiasing Strategy: Use pre-mortem to stress-test confidence. Assign explicit confidence levels and track calibration over time. Seek outside perspectives.
Example: A project manager estimates delivery in 3 months with 90% confidence, despite a track record of similar projects taking 5-7 months.

Keywords: framing, gain frame, loss frame, presentation, wording, perspective, positive negative, context dependent
Description: Reaching different conclusions from the same information depending on how it is presented — as a gain vs. a loss, as a percentage vs. absolute number.
Impact on Decisions: The way options are framed (gain vs. loss, survival vs. mortality) systematically shifts preferences even when the underlying facts are identical.
How to Detect: Ask: Would our preference change if this were framed differently? Are we reacting to the framing or the substance?
Debiasing Strategy: Deliberately reframe the decision both as gains and as losses. Check if the conclusion changes. Present data in multiple formats.
Example: A treatment described as having a 90% survival rate is preferred over one described as having a 10% mortality rate, though they are identical.

Keywords: availability, vivid, recent, memorable, anecdote, easily recalled, dramatic, salient event
Description: Overweighting information that is easily recalled (vivid, recent, emotionally charged) when estimating probabilities or making judgments.
Impact on Decisions: Recent dramatic events distort probability estimates. A vivid failure story outweighs statistical base rates in our judgment.
How to Detect: Ask: Are we basing this estimate on systematic data or on memorable anecdotes? Is a recent event disproportionately influencing our assessment?
Debiasing Strategy: Use base rates from systematic data rather than anecdotes. Seek statistical evidence. Ask: what does the aggregate data say, not just the vivid examples?
Example: After a highly publicized startup failure, investors become disproportionately risk-averse about an entire sector despite strong aggregate returns.

Keywords: groupthink, conformity, consensus, dissent suppressed, harmony, team pressure, unanimity illusion
Description: A group converges on a preferred option without critical evaluation because members prioritize harmony and consensus over honest assessment.
Impact on Decisions: The group fails to surface dissenting views, alternative options, or risk information. Creates an illusion of unanimity that masks true disagreement.
How to Detect: Ask: Has anyone expressed strong disagreement? Are we reaching consensus suspiciously fast? Are quieter team members actually aligned or just silent?
Debiasing Strategy: Use structured dissent techniques: pre-mortem, red team, anonymous input. Assign a devil's advocate. Create psychological safety for disagreement.
Example: A leadership team unanimously approves an acquisition despite private doubts from several members who feel unable to challenge the CEO's enthusiasm.

Keywords: planning fallacy, optimistic timeline, underestimate, cost overrun, schedule slip, best case thinking
Description: Systematically underestimating the time, cost, and risk of future actions while overestimating their benefits, even when aware of similar past overruns.
Impact on Decisions: The chosen option appears more attractive than it actually is because its costs and timelines are underestimated. Distorts option comparison.
How to Detect: Ask: Are our estimates based on best-case assumptions? Have similar projects in the past taken longer or cost more than planned?
Debiasing Strategy: Use reference class forecasting — compare to actual outcomes of similar past decisions rather than building bottom-up estimates from scratch.
Example: A team estimates a software migration will take 4 months and cost $200K. Similar past migrations averaged 9 months and $500K.

Keywords: loss aversion, risk averse, fear of loss, prospect theory, downside, potential loss weighs more, regret
Description: Weighing potential losses approximately twice as heavily as equivalent gains, leading to excessive risk avoidance and status quo preference.
Impact on Decisions: Decision-makers reject options with positive expected value because the potential downside feels disproportionately painful compared to the upside.
How to Detect: Ask: Are we avoiding this option primarily because of what we might lose, rather than evaluating the full picture? Would we accept this bet if offered as a gain?
Debiasing Strategy: Reframe in terms of opportunity cost — what do we lose by NOT acting? Evaluate gains and losses on the same scale. Consider the cost of inaction.
Example: A company refuses to enter a promising new market because the potential $2M loss looms larger than the expected $5M gain.

Keywords: recency, recent events, short-term memory, latest data, recent trend, extrapolation, last quarter
Description: Overweighting recent events and trends when evaluating options, while ignoring longer-term base rates and historical patterns.
Impact on Decisions: A recent success or failure with a similar approach disproportionately influences the current decision, regardless of long-term track record.
How to Detect: Ask: Are we extrapolating from the last few data points? Would our assessment change if we looked at 5-10 years of data instead of the last quarter?
Debiasing Strategy: Extend the time horizon of analysis. Look at 5-10 year data, not just the recent period. Explicitly seek base rates across full business cycles.
Example: After two strong quarters, a team extrapolates growth and approves aggressive expansion, ignoring the cyclical nature of their industry.

Keywords: survivorship, success stories, invisible failures, selection bias, winners only, survivor, hidden data
Description: Drawing conclusions from successful examples while ignoring failures that followed the same approach but are no longer visible.
Impact on Decisions: Options appear more promising than they are because we only see the winners and not the many failures that used the same strategy.
How to Detect: Ask: Are we only looking at successful cases? Who tried this approach and failed? What happened to the alternatives we aren't seeing?
Debiasing Strategy: Actively seek failed examples of each option under consideration. Ask: who tried this and failed, and why? Analyze the full population, not just survivors.
Example: A team studies 5 successful companies that used a particular strategy, without examining the 50 companies that used the same strategy and went bankrupt.
